sentences.text: So he called his girlfriend.
And in this sort of larking about, he tells her, look, have you got a young girl for these-- two young girls for these two boys?
And she says, sure, yeah.
I know just the girl.
And she comes back with one.
So then they say, well, she'll have to choose between the two of you which one she likes better, you know.
And we were not too near each other.
We were like 50 yards.
So she didn't-- but she said she liked me as a boyfriend.
Oh, what sort of boyfriend it is, you know. 50 yards, you can call across the wire.
And suddenly the SS man who is in charge of the Kleiderkammer comes around-- not the officer.
The-- the ranks of the SS were handling prisoners.
All the beating was done by the ranks up to Sergeant, all right.
Sergeant is scharfuhrer.
Oberscharfuhrer is Sergeant.
Then comes officers-- lieutenant, and so on, and sturmbannfuhrer.
The SS had their own ranks.
So it wasn't the officer.
It was the scharfuhrer.
He comes with a stick.
And he says, I'm going to punish you for this [GERMAN]-- immoral behavior.
Immoral behavior.
We were calling across the wire to girls who were 50 feet away, you know, across the road and across two-- two sets of-- two-- two fences.
But he doesn't punish Rudy.
Because Rudy and him have got a trade relationship, you see.
So he knows that Rudy has girlfriends.
And he shuts his eyes to this.
The immoral behavior is by these two boys.
So-- oh, we were allowed to grow our hair by this time, these two boys.
The others had their hair shorn.
But because we were special prisoners, we were allowed to grow hair.
I have to tell you about that.
It's-- that's the story that concerns Mengele and other-- other things.
So we already had a-- a shock of hair, you know, quite nicely.
And we were very proud, too.
It makes you feel really good.
So he tells me to bend down.
Now I was-- I-- I-- I was beaten once in Theresienstadt when we weren't working in the gardens.
In Theresienstadt, Freddy-- Freddy Hirsch arranged that the youth who were sort of already grown up worked in gardens on the-- on the walls outside Theresienstadt.
And the produce of the gardens-- you know, fresh produce-- was given to the small children because of vitamin B and all kinds of carrots were grown a lot.
So we worked in the gardens.
And the kids got it.
We could steal a few carrots.
And I walked after a day's work in the gardens.
And I had a cap on.
And an SS man saw me-- that's in Theresienstadt.
And you're supposed to doff your cap, you see, when you walk past.
And I didn't doff my cap.
So he shouts, come here and gives me one.
You know, he says [GERMAN]-- bend down.
And they have these inch sticks-- walking sticks-- and he'd give you one with the walking stick.
And I wet myself.
I was terribly ashamed that I wet myself.
And all the other boys were very solicitous, you know.
But I wet myself.
So I told myself-- you know, he said [GERMAN] to me.
And I told myself, I'm going to hold and I'm not going to wet myself.
And I wet myself.
It's-- the bladder has its own mind.
But I got three.
The other boy-- and I don't remember his name.
I think I recognized him as.
But he doesn't remember working.
Or he puts it away from me-- him.
I know why.
He saw me being beaten.
And I said, for heaven's sakes, bend down, hold.
And he sort of moved each time.
So each time the stroke didn't land properly.
And the more he went sideways, and the more he cried, the more this-- this fellow couldn't have enough.
He-- he beat the living daylight out of him.
You know, he beat him everywhere.
Thereafter, all the ranks of the SS were complaining to the officers that the boys are running about the camp creating trouble.
Yes.
And there is this story that I made you read at the beginning about [?
Durlacher ?] when one of the SS nearly drowned him.
Because they were bathing in the pond which was meant for the fire brigade-- huh, fire brigade.
That whole story of [?
Durlacher's-- ?] and pond for the fire brigade.
You know that-- they had a pond.
And they had-- they had life-saving apparatus.
Well, I mean I saw it.
So all these events.
And they saw these boys running around the camp and begging food from the other prisoners.
So it-- it sort of disorganized the camp according to the ranks.
And they said, we have a responsibility for dealing with the prisoners.
But the officers somehow said, look, it's none of your business.
But there was a selection that half the boys would go to a farm and work on a farm.
And the boys could volunteer to go.
And this fool of a boy who got beaten and who was with me in the Kleiderkammer volunteered and went.
Now either it's him and he doesn't want to remember his beating-- but I think he volunteered because he felt he lost face.
Well, when I questioned the other Birkenau boys, nobody was on the farm.
Nobody knows about the farm.
I know about the farm because I got it solely through the scuttlebutt, through the gossip, the camp gossip, sort of.
I think somehow they were killed or in the death marches.
Anyway, nobody who survives remembers going on the farm.
What happened to us both was that this fellow who beat us had us report.
And he cut a swathe through our hair with scissors, you know.
He's got these big tailor's scissors and he-- because the hair made us feel good.
So he cut a swathe through just with the scissors.
It's like a, you know, punishment on top of punishment.
And I mean, we didn't do anything.
We stood in a place where we were not supposed to be.
We talked to other prisoners in another compound, which we were not supposed to do.
But so what?
You know.
Rudy was a friend of his-- well, not friend.
But Rudy and him had some sort of business relationship with contraband.
Rudy, incidentally, told me that he could roll through the wire to see his girlfriend.
Now he told me that he could roll through the wire.
He'd have to cross the road.
The wire was lit, apparently electrified.

So he had arrangements with the electricians to switch off the current, roll through one lot of barbed wire, go over the road-- which is watched by a tower where there's a man with a machine gun-- roll through another wire in order to visit his girlfriend in C compound.
And I didn't believe him.
I-- but next morning he says, I spent the night with my girlfriend.
And I rolled through the wire.
And there wasn't a mark on him, you know.
There wasn't a scratch.
So I said, how did you do it?
How do you roll through the wire?
And I don't know how they roll through the wire.
When I talked to the block altester, he said that there was a sewage outfall that people had access to.
If you paid the right people in the prison, you got a key to get into the sewage outlet.
Perhaps he went through the sewage outlet.
There was a way of doing it.
I don't know how it was done.
I have another instance of this that I saw.
And I can't explain it either.
That was when we were still in the Familienlager.
A man came after [? appel ?] and during the time of the liquidation.
And he had some sort of urgent message for the lager altester.
And suddenly after [? appel ?] it was dusk.
And there is this strange man, obviously a permanent-- you know, one of these uniform people.
Completely clean, you know.
Unbelievable that he would go through a sewage outlet or something.
But he was completely clean.
He says, can you go to block number 7 where the lager altester lived-- his name was Willie-- and tell him and his girlfriend that I'd like to speak to them.
And see that you're not watched, or, you know-- quietly.
When-- when I brought them, they came straight away, as if they knew he was-- it was like by appointment.
And they came and talked to him at the end of the camp.
It was already night time.
And they told me to watch out, sort of-- go a couple of blocks.
And when I whistle, you come back.
He whistled.
And the lager altester walked past me with his girlfriend-- Jewish girlfriend-- that he lived with.
And the man said, is the coast clear?
So he says, go back to the corner of the building and see that nobody's coming.
And, like, I went two minutes later to see where he was and he had disappeared.
When I talk to-- to Rudy [?

Rodin, ?] who is the block altester of Krankenbau, and I said to him, how was this done, he said, it must have been done through the sewage outlet and he must have been one of the people who had keys to the sewage outlet.
No-- so-- no, my story.
How was-- how were we allowed to grow our hair?
A new officer came to be in charge of the Kleiderkammer.
Now it's in the Maennerlager.
It's about--
August-- August '44-- August-- yeah, August.
A new officer comes and he's very smart-- Von Schwartzenberg.
And he was obersturmbannfuhrer.
He had a higher rank than Hoss, who was the commandant of Auschwitz.
He was very high ranking.
Came from Berlin.
Only to do with Winterhilfe.
Because we were sending boxes of clothes to Germany.
All the clothes that was taken off the Jews that came to Auschwitz was shipped back to Germany for people who were bombed.
So people got linen.
And everything that was brought to Auschwitz was handed out to German families who were bombed.
Well, I'm going to describe this to you.
He comes-- new man, very imposing figure.
And I had-- you know, it was a sinecure, I told you.
So I was helping out in the office where the records were kept.
And there was a Jewish man from Salonika.
I don't remember his name.
He was the [? schreiber. ?] And his files were in dreadful disorder, you know, of these shipments.
But he kept writing each shipment on cards.
And there was supposed to be a card index.
So he said, well, he ought to be organize such and such.
He had a mountain of work there.
So I said, look, I'll help you-- can I-- can I help in the office?
So I spent most of the day there actually sorting out the office and the filing.
And within about a week, his office was-- I mean, I'd never done filing before.
I didn't know what it was.
But we cleared it all up.
And this Von Schwartzenberg comes in and he starts asking a question about shipments, trainloads of clothes that went to Berlin-- the dates and so on.
So the [? schreiber ?] says, the Lauefer [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- the runner knows where everything is filed.
He'll find you the card.
And the reason was because he didn't know the answer.
[LAUGHTER] You see.
I found the card, showed him the card.
And it was all clearly-- clearly written.
He said, good.
And he's going out of the office.
And he-- sort of like a second thought, he turns back and opens the door.
And he stands in the door.
And he says to me, do you know what goes on here?
So I say, yes.
I know what's goes on here.
So he says, but do you know what really goes on here?
And he sort of makes with his chin to the North.
So I say, yes, I do know what goes on here.
What was he referring to?
Well, to the crematoria, I thought, and to these clothes-- you know, where do all these clothes come from.
So I say, yes, I know what goes on here-- the second time.
So he says, where do all these people go?
And now, you know, this was an unknown-- this man.
He just came.
He's asking a few questions.
He's being normally polite.
But you never know with these people.
And, I mean, I'm-- I'm now fairly aware of what's happening.
So I was then as well.
I said to him, they go to a better place than here.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So he says, [? ach, ?] [? ya. ?] And he is going to shut the door.
And he says, I want to bring you something tomorrow.
Do you need anything?
To me.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
And that was still when I didn't have hair, you see.
So I said, if you can, I'd like permission to grow my hair.
And he answered me-- and he was a new boy, you know, a new officer, and a very high rank.
And he says to me, I will look it up in standing orders.
That's why I keep going on about standing orders.
I wonder if the Russians ever found any standing orders in any of the camps.
I've asked historians of the Holocaust about it.
Nobody knows anything about standing orders.
And there must have been such a-- such a document.
Because somewhere the document must have come into Allied hands.
There must be some place where somebody can find such a document.
He comes back a couple of days later and he says-- I mean, it's-- it's-- it's-- it-- the way he said it, it's so bizarre.
He said, I have made an application to Berlin and I have spoken to Sturmbannfuhrer Hoss.
And it will be all right.
You can grow your hair, because usually permission is given.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And that's how we grew our hair.
And he said the other boy can grow his hair as well.
And then we got beaten.
For-- for this lark, you know, that we were talking across the fence.
And then there was the selection and half the boys disappeared.
Including this other boy.
And then I am on my way back.
And it all happened very quickly, you know-- a week later perhaps, I was running from some sort of errand at Kanada towards the Kleiderkammer.
And it was summer time, you know, August.
And got hot and bothered.
And I didn't notice.
I-- I saw two uniforms, but I sort of didn't pay any attention.
I kept running.
And then as I was running, I realized that Mengele and this Von Schwartzenberg were standing together-- Schwartzenberg, Von Schwartzenberg-- were standing together chatting.
And I was told to keep out of Mengele's way.
But it was too late, you know.
I was sort of running towards the gate of the Kleiderkammer where I was supposed to report.
And they were standing right in front of the door.
So I stopped running and I stood at attention.
I said, permission to-- you know, I asked for permission to go into the Kleiderkammer.
And Mengele says, still stehen-- still stehen is the command 'stand still,' you know, at attention-- like stand at attention.
So they are chatting and I'm standing at attention.
And they are discussing art.
If you can imagine this situation, these belching chimneys, you know.
And I came hot and bothered.
And I didn't notice that there are these two officers.
And I sort of like walked-- walking into a trap.
I mean, it was open ground.
It was my fault.
I should have stopped running 2-- or 300 yards earlier.
But I stand at attention.
And they're discussing art.
And I can't quite hear, because I stand a little bit further away.
And I'm told to stand at attention.
So I stand like glued to where I am standing.
And they turn and they walk towards the west of the camp, you know, towards the railway tracks, which was the-- the-- the-- away from the gate, you see.
And Mengele sort of turns and says, [GERMAN]-- command, you're coming with us.
So I follow.
So they talk.
And I had been there before.
The-- one of the storage barracks on the other side of the football field in the compound was handed over to a painter by the SS.
And this man was given paints.
And he was painting pictures.
And it was an interesting place to go.
And I-- I wasn't very busy.
And I had the run of the camp.
So I went to visit there.
And I talked to him quite a few times.
And he was a Lithuanian Jew, was an artist, some famous--
He was painting his family.
All the pictures were all portraits of his wife, his boys, and his little girl.
They were all the same.
And he kept-- as the canvases went, he painted the same thing.
And he-- you know, all the prisoners told you the same story.
They had been there a long time, and this, and that.
The stories were all the same.
So you sort of got bored with it all, because it's-- it's all the same.
It's-- the fact that it varies a little bit here and there is of no consequence, because it's all the same.
It's all-- it's all this grinding death.
And this man is quite mad, because he's-- he's talking about losing his family all the time.
You know, he's not reconciled.
And we're walking into his [INAUDIBLE] atelier-- into the-- his atelier.
So he had a whole barrack.
And it was all sunny, you know, in the summer.
Beautiful for painting.
And they chat about art, which I didn't understand.
I mean, I didn't know all these things about Bach, or Mozart, and so on.
They could have talked about China for all I-- for all I knew.
Because I was a lad of 14 who hadn't been to school for four years.
So Mengele says to this officer Schwartzenberg, he says, tell you what.
Since you're going to the front, I'll get this painter to paint him.
He points at me.
And then he gives the command to the painter.
And he says, you're gonna make a portrait of him and we're going to send it to Berlin to the apartment of Obersturmbannfuhrer Von Schwartzenberg.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
A present.
But the significant thing was that Schwartzenberg had been with us some 10 days.
In that time, he organized for me to grow my hair.
And Mengele tried to impress him about his civilization, and his sort of interesting art, and this music.
They were talking about the orchestra at the gate.
And this painter, you know, he's established a painter to paint pictures, because we don't kill artists.
But Herting told me that the people in the SS who are in the command of looking after prisoners are themselves prisoners, because they will never do anything else except work with prisoners.
Because this is all secret.
And the only way that they can get out of this prison detail is to volunteer for the front.
And this Von Schwartzenberg volunteered for the front rather than stay in some sort of sinecure in Auschwitz.
Yes, once more.
Anyway, my portrait is painted over another two or three weeks, through August.
And there's a fire.
And the man-- the painter-- put a light to all the paints and he burned everything and committed suicide in the fire.
And this sort of topsy-turvy logic, you know, of this camp-- every barrack had a fire extinguisher.
So the command is called from the gate-- every barrack, you know, fire extinguishers to the fire.
So Nitka grabs the fire extinguisher and carries it.
And I run with Nitka.
You know, fire, it's an event, you know.
And our scharfuhrer, the fellow who by-- by now had cut-- cut the swathe through my slowly-growing hair, is running behind us to the fire.
And Nitka, poor chap-- I told you he was like a thread; he wasn't very strong-- he hands me the fire extinguisher and he says, I can't anymore.
You-- you run with the fire extinguisher.
And I was a small boy.
And this was a big thing, this fire extinguisher, and heavy.
So I couldn't carry it.
And I ran a few steps and then I slung it over my shoulder.
[LAUGHS] What I didn't know was that this was one of these fire extinguishers that you-- when you put it upside down, it activates.
So I was running--
[LAUGHS]
